title: Patrícia Contreiras -Hiring Manager Interview 1 - Andi | AI Content Manager
meeting_title: Patrícia Contreiras -Hiring Manager Interview 1 - Andi | AI Content Manager

transcript.text: Sorry for a couple of minutes of being late.
This meeting is being recorded.
The Zoom website was a bit wonky.
It wasn't working properly.
Ah, okay.
Give me just a second.
I'm logging in this morning.
Well, anyways, now we're both here.
It's Monday.
Here we go.
Okay, I can give you like a quick overview on this role and kind of how it works within GrowthX.
And then we'll spend most of the time talking about you.
And then from there, yeah, I'll try and leave a little bit of time for questions.
But this role, content manager.
So GrowthX is essentially a services organization.
We're building marketing content for like at scale using our own internal AI platform.
And that means that a content manager at GrowthX is really running AI systems and our AI platform to generate content at scale.
So you'll be generating 10 to 20 pieces of content per week, doing light edits, and then sharing them with a managing editor who will then do a second round of edits and share them with a client.
So two skill sets here.
Obviously, we want somebody with like strong editorial background, like who's spent a lot of time generating content.
And then the other side is just working on the platform itself.
So experience with AI, experience using AI to generate content, some experience with prompting and the ability to tweak prompts and continue to like get the content that you want leveraging AI.
And...
Yeah, we're growing super fast, so we're really in need of these kinds of roles.
And so, yeah, I'd just love to hear about you.
Basically, I do a little bit of all of that.
I started doing copywriting around 11, 12 years ago, but officially 10 years ago when I moved to Europe.
I lived between Portugal and London for 10 years.
And there I started copywriting.
But at the end of this career at this company, which was Farfetch and e-commerce, I wrote product technical details.
I wrote editorial.
I had to do a little bit of editing, especially because when I joined, it was such a small department, the content department.
So I had the privilege to learn a bit of everything.
And then towards the end of this role, when I...
I no longer saw myself in the company.
I started dabbling into AI.
I started doing freelance work for the US as an AI trainer.
There's tons of different titles for that.
And that's when I learned more about the behind the scenes of AI.
And I had to do proper, create proper prompt criterias and follow certain rules to train the AI to get the best results we could.
And then when I got here, back to Brazil, I started doing my own projects.
I thought, well, I have some extra time right now in my hands, so I'm going to get busy and translate those projects into my professional life.
And then started doing blogging, doing blog posts more with an informational tone.
And I started using...
using AI for that.
I used a lot of chat dpt and some Grammarly for grammar, punctuation, and editing tone of voice, but then I started researching more about other AI tools that I didn't know about.
I tried Cod, I tried Gemini, until I learned that I do prefer chat dpt between all of those.
I also started meddling a bit with perplexity.
And then when I was thinking about a content pipeline, which would be the perfect way to scale content, because personally, I do more of an editorial, a lot of my tone of voice.
But I know for companies, it's completely different.
You have to pick different brands, different tones of voices, different writing styles.
So when I thought of that, I started using the chat dpt, and then perplexity, and then going to Grammarly.
it.
Then thinking about a tool that everyone could use, I put that Trello, but I think Notion's really great right now.
I've been using it for a while.
And then the basic, which is Word documents, which is very easy and everyone has access to.
So basically, that's me in a wrap.
Yeah, yeah.
How do you think about, like, or how has your process changed in the time since you've been using AI?
Like, I'd love some more detail there.
It got faster.
And no doubt about that.
Even though we have to do little tweaks and go back and forth tons of times, because AI is not perfect, it's never going to replace humans.
And for me, that's a great thing, or else I would be unemployed.
Yeah.
And I think instead of being scared of AI and learning...
recovery needs...
I love
And believing what the market's like, we're going out of jobs because AI is still in our jobs.
No, I really have been seeing AI tools as a way of improving my work and making it faster and helping me develop what I want to develop.
It is what it is.
They are tools.
AI tools are tools.
They are not replacing anything.
So basically how I use for my private projects is I use it to, hey, I know this topic.
I need this topic for this public.
Give me some ideas of what I could write about based on the subject.
And then it gives me some ideas and then we have some exchanges and then I ask for a first draft.

I go back, I edit it, I edit some of my own editorial into it, that's basically it, I think it got faster, gives me ideas, helps me brainstorm, especially as it's a very lonely work, writing and editing, don't really deal with a lot of people to do that, so yeah, it's basically a tool.

Yeah, yeah, I mean, like, start to finish, like, I know you talked a little bit, like, are you using it in the editorial process afterwards, like, what kinds of prompts are you thinking about, or, you know, what are you finding that it's especially, I know you said brainstorming and a couple of other things, but just would love like some more specific examples of how you've used it.
So, for blog posts, I usually search on Google Trends, I don't for example, the, the, take,
I go there and ask about apps for drivers, for companies that offer that service.
And then I try a few different keywords on Google Trends to make sure it's trending or else it's not going to get anywhere with SEO.
And then I get all those details and the data based on the U.S.
market.
Pick the states that are trending that keyword more and use all of that into ChatGPT, if it's the case of using ChatGPT or any other LMM.
And I basically say like, hey, you are a professional content specialist and you have to write a blog post for this specific topic.
It needs to be U.S.
centered, especially in the States, da-da-da.
And, um...
And it needs to have at least three H2s, which is second headings and a main heading, and it needs to have a clear structured layout, like bullet points.
And I had a work count.
I try to be very specific with the prompts, or else it's going to give me stuff that sometimes I don't need, and I have to go back and forth for nothing.
It makes not much of sense.
And after that, I ask it to generate a first draft.
And then after that, it's going to give me the first draft.
I'm going to read everything.
Then I remove what I don't need.
And if that is relevant, I go to it and I say, hey, this bit is good, but give me this and this and that.
It's still missing this.

And again, I can reveal everything, and everything, and once I'm completely okay with it, I use perplexity to check facts, make sure everything is right there, because perplexity brings the proper links to make sure that information is really there, just doesn't say like, oh, it's okay, believe me.
No, it's bringing me proper fact checks there.
And I check everything, because sometimes it's not completely right.
For example, for this take home, it told me you have this and that company, but this and that company were from Europe.
It's not relevant, it's not going to affect the US market.
So I had to remove that, even though perplexity told me it was fine, it could go.
And then go to Grimm.

Emily, to make sure everything is in the same tone, everything's following the same writing style, check for minor spellings or punctuation, and then straight to whatever document editor that is used, and a calendar, so everyone is aware of what's going on.
Yeah, yeah.
Awesome.
Thank you.
That was like, exactly what I was looking for.
Perfect.
Awesome.
Well, one more question just for me is, you know, can you talk to me about how you, like, think about the definition of done and setting the bar high?
Like, you know, you talked a little bit about having a couple of, like, going through some versions and pushing back, but like, how do you set kind of the goal for what your content should look like?
Like, how do you think about, okay, this is.
Good enough.
First, I think that I'm very into quality.
Because while working for a big e-commerce, we had really high targets and it was getting higher and higher.
And sometimes they would say, like, just prioritize the numbers.
We need to deliver the numbers.
And I was like, it's also really important having quality or else we're going to be constantly editing everything and doing all this.
And so for me, it's basically creating a list, a checklist.
It needs to have this.
For example, I don't know if this role is going to prioritize SEO as well or if someone else is going to be doing SEO.
So it needs to have a proper structure so it's easier to be found on Google and ranking there.
And then what is it, the basics that I want?
I want them.
All the headings, I want the subtitles, I want the meta, I want the hyperlinks, I want all of that.

And for example, how many hyperlinks, how many headings, make sure everything is written down, so I can go back and check everything, because it's a long process, so it's important to have everything down for checking.

And also adding, what are the main subjects about the topic, the take home, do I have to bring in companies that do this service, I have to have the links for these companies, I have to have a little introduction and explanation what it does.
Well, considering it's a public that already knows what it is, because it's very specific, and I had no idea what it did, just a basic explanation, not getting too much into detail.
Or else you're going to sound a little arrogant, maybe.
So guiding the reader step by step, what you could do, how you can get there.
This platform, this website is good because of it.
And doing maybe tables of pros and cons.
So writing all of that is really important to make sure I did all of this.
I don't know, putting maybe a maximum of edits and reviews.
Or else you'll never find a perfect article, not for writers.
We're going to still think like, no, this is not good.
We could put it there.
So maybe setting a number of times that could go back and forth.
Especially for people like me that really want this perfectly reading thing.
So yeah, I think that's the main thing.
And once you have everything...
You make sure the grammar is good, all facts are all right, everything is fine in like veracity, you can go ahead.
During my experience, I learned that I can't really get attached too much about perfectionism, because there's a deadline and you have to know how to balance it.
Yeah, yeah.
Awesome.
Cool.
Well, that, those are all the questions I had.
Is there, what questions do you have for me?
Well, I wanted to know more about the role, because I did a take home before, when it was focused on something different.
Oh, yeah.
I noticed the title changed.
Also, Marilia told me some details, such as the experience, the two-month experience was also added.
So I was thinking...
Did anything change in the role itself, like day-to-day, the specifics, responsibilities, from what I talked to with Matthew the last time, or everything's the same?
Pretty much everything's the same.
I think we found that we were going a little bit fast in the hiring process, which is why there was like that break.
And we wanted to just judge for a little bit, like a couple of additional pieces, because we found that, yeah, some people just weren't as successful in the role.
So the role is the same.
The expectations are what we expected, like the first time that we hired.
We just updated the process and the title so that we could really clarify what we were looking for.
I think we learned a lot in the first round of hiring.
And so, you know, we're taking a second round, like some of this is growth, some of this is just backfills, some people weren't a good fit to start.
And so that's why we're, this approach is a little bit different than the first time, like the initial start of the process that you jumped into.
Okay.
And you mentioned SEO.
I was expecting that because that's how I work, but I wasn't sure because Matthew didn't talk about it.
And I think the details of the role also didn't mention it.
But aside from drafting, writing, editing, and SEO, are there any other people other than the second editor and final editor to like collaborate in the process?
Yeah.

So I think SEO is like a very nice to have for us because part of this role, like in the drafting and editing, we're also doing like keyword research so that we can come up with like bulk content, a bulk content strategy.
So maybe we'll say like, okay, these are like 50 topics, like using the keywords that.

We think are opportunities, and so I think that's mostly the SEO, and then like there is some, you know, if you know how to do like a full SEO audit on a website, that's like an amazing plus, but there is an account strategist or account director that's overseeing 10-ish accounts, so you'll be working on one to two, your managing editor will be working with one, with like two or three content managers who are working on one to two accounts, so they'll be overseeing a few more accounts, and then an account director will kind of help on a like monthly or quarterly basis to come in and say, okay, like, let's look at what we've done, let's maybe think about changing the direction a little bit, or look at some of the signals, and how can we change this, like, how can we think about the next few months of this account?
So there's kind of an account strategist that's thinking long-term about performance, a managing editor who's like project
They're, like, project managing and editing.
So, like, okay, we're, like, coordinating all the pieces.
And then you're really thinking deeply about, like, piece to piece, what's the output.
So I think that's, like, how we think about the support.
So, because sometimes it's hard to be, like, so focused on something and then take that step back and think, like, high level.
And so that's how we think about, like, account support is you're thinking about, like, the quality, the baseline quality bar for everything that goes out.
managing editor is thinking about, like, across the project.
And then the account strategist is thinking about, like, okay, are there different work streams that we're working on or things like that.
Okay.
And so basically from what I get, the company is acting sort of like an agency working for other companies.
And then when we get another company, a client, we get a brief.
with everything like a brand, their own brand on a voice and all of that, or do we have just the meetings to set expectations and what do they expect?
So the managing editor leads like weekly meetings, and the content manager really doesn't need to attend those meetings.

They're, we're in a shared Slack channel with clients, and so really that's like the main, and then we also have an air table, which is like our project management tool, so that's how we're engaging with clients, in terms of like content approval and things like that, and then they're working and we're working in Word for like comments and edits.
And so like, yeah, I think the main tool that you'll use to, to talk to clients is Slack.
Okay, and And
How big is the team?
Just from curiosity.
Yeah, I think there are like 40-ish people on the delivery team.
And that's like the client-facing side is the delivery team.
I think we have like 60 or 70 overall.
Wow, that's a lot of people.
Yeah.
Okay.
I think that's it from my end, at least out of my head.
I think that's it.
All right.
Well, Patricia, thank you so much for your time.
It was great to meet you.
And yeah, I think Marilia will be in touch with Next Steps.
Okay.
Thank you, Andi.
Have a good one.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Hiring manager interview for AI Content Manager role at GrowthX.](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Role involves generating 10-20 pieces of content weekly using AI, light editing, and collaborating with managing editors](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=46.0)
  - [Patricia has 10+ years of copywriting experience and recent AI content generation expertise](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=133.0)
  - [GrowthX operates as a service agency, creating marketing content at scale for clients using their internal AI platform](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=49.0)
  - [The role requires strong editorial background, AI experience, and prompt engineering skills](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=87.0)

## Topics

### Role Overview and Requirements

  - [Content manager runs AI systems to generate content at scale (10-20 pieces/week)](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=60.0)
  - [Light editing before sharing with managing editor](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=71.0)
  - [Key skills: Strong editorial background, AI experience, prompt engineering](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=87.0)
  - [Company growing rapidly, high demand for this role](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=120.0)

### Patricia's Background and Experience

  - [10+ years copywriting experience (Farfetch, e-commerce)](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=133.0)
  - [Recent focus on AI content generation and training](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=182.0)
  - [Freelance AI trainer for US companies](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=186.0)
  - [Personal projects using ChatGPT, Grammarly, Claude, Gemini, Perplexity](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=242.0)
  - [Developed content pipeline for scaling: ChatGPT \> Perplexity \> Grammarly \> Trello/Notion \> Word](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=268.0)

### AI-Assisted Content Creation Process

  - [Uses Google Trends for keyword research and topic selection](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=470.0)
  - [Detailed prompting in ChatGPT for initial draft](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=507.0)
  - [Iterative refinement with AI](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=584.0)
  - [Fact-checking with Perplexity](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=600.0)
  - [Grammar and tone adjustment with Grammarly](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=660.0)
  - [Final edits and formatting in document editor](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=660.0)

### Quality Assurance and "Definition of Done"

  - [Creates detailed checklists for content requirements](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=749.0)
  - [Focuses on proper structure, headings, meta info, hyperlinks](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=767.0)
  - [Balances perfectionism with deadlines](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=720.0)
  - [Sets maximum number of edits/reviews to avoid endless tweaking](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=866.0)

### Team Structure and Collaboration

  - [Content managers work on 1-2 accounts](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=1059.0)
  - [Managing editors oversee 2-3 content managers](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=1059.0)
  - [Account directors/strategists manage \~10 accounts, provide long-term strategy](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=1074.0)
  - [40-ish people on delivery team, 60-70 overall in company](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=1264.0)

### Client Interaction and Tools

  - [Shared Slack channels with clients](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=1217.0)
  - [Airtable for project management](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=1227.0)
  - [Microsoft Word for comments and edits](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=1227.0)
  - [Weekly client meetings led by managing editors (content managers typically don't attend)](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=1217.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Marilia to follow up with Patricia regarding next steps in the hiring process](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=1288.0)
  - [Patricia to await further communication about potential advancement in the selection process](https://fathom.video/share/sqUVgRGUtTKX-Y538PTWqbxhysDLE_jz?tab=summary&timestamp=1298.0)
